extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) requires that no reachable state ever has opted_out=true AND sent=true. Transition t5 directly enables such a state: on send_attempt, if (opted_out AND ticks>=10), it sets sent:=true. This is reachable.

Counterexample execution (12 events, within the 14-event bound):
1. tick x10: ticks climbs 0->10 (capped at 10), opted_out stays false, sent stays false. State: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=10.
2. optout (t1): opted_out:=true, sent:=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=10.
3. send_attempt: guard of t5 (opted_out AND ticks>=10) is satisfied, so t5 fires: sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=10.

At this state opted_out=true and sent=true, so (opted_out implies not sent) is FALSE, violating the global property.

Note on rule interaction: on the final send_attempt both t3 (send_attempt if opted_out -> sent:=false) and t5 (send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=10 -> sent:=true) have satisfied guards. t5 is the strictly more specific rule (it adds the ticks>=10 condition that t3 lacks) and is explicitly authored to set sent:=true under opted_out, which is precisely the contradiction the property forbids. Under any priority scheme that resolves the conflict toward the more specific rule, the violating state is reachable. Since the question asks whether SOME execution can violate the property and t5 makes the violating state reachable, the property is violated. t1/t2/t4 all set sent:=false and cannot independently cause the violation; only t5 produces opted_out=true with sent=true, and the execution above reaches its guard.
